Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
South Asian Traditions

Haribhadra

8th c. CE

A Jain philosopher of yoga, comparison, and consciousness under karmic obstruction.

How does an intrinsically conscious self become limited, deluded, embodied, affectively distorted, morally bound, and epistemically partial; and how can that same self be purified into unobstructed knowledge, non-delusion, and liberation?

The answer is Jain in its metaphysical base but Haribhadrian in its comparative and psychological articulation. The self, or jīva, is an enduring conscious reality. Consciousness, knowledge, and perception are not accidental products of matter but intrinsic capacities of the self. Ordinary mental life, however, is not pure consciousness. It is consciousness under conditions: karmic obstruction, sensory mediation, passion, attachment, aversion, delusion, habit, embodiment, and perspectival limitation. Liberation is therefore not the manufacture of consciousness but the removal of obstructions to consciousness.

This produces a distinctive philosophy of mind with several major commitments: realism about conscious selves; non-reductionism about consciousness; a karmic account of cognitive limitation; a moral psychology in which affect and cognition are inseparable; a developmental model of mental purification; an anti-Cartesian but strongly dualist mind-body theory; and a many-sided epistemology that explains why finite knowers grasp partial truths rather than the whole.

1. Historical and textual position

Haribhadra stands at an important intersection in Indian intellectual history. He inherits the classical Jain framework of soul, karma, bondage, and liberation; he writes after major Buddhist, Brahmanical, Jain, and yoga systems had already reached high sophistication; and he becomes one of the major Jain figures to organize these systems comparatively. His Ṣaḍdarśanasamuccaya is associated with the comparative presentation of six philosophical systems, while his Śāstravārttāsamuccaya surveys competing doctrines, including rival accounts of causality and liberation. He is therefore not merely a sectarian theologian but a systematic doxographer: someone who understands philosophy by placing doctrines into structured comparison.

For philosophy of mind, the most important Haribhadrian materials are his yoga works, especially Yogabindu, Yogadṛṣṭisamuccaya, Yogaśataka, and Yogaviṃśikā. The Yogabindu and Yogadṛṣṭisamuccaya are especially important because they show how Haribhadra connects liberation, cognition, affect, conduct, and contemplative transformation. The Yogabindu defines yoga broadly as what leads to mokṣa, and Haribhadra interprets the Jain path of right faith, right knowledge, and right conduct in a deliberately non-sectarian but still metaphysically serious way.

The Yogadṛṣṭisamuccaya, a Sanskrit work of 228 verses, presents the ascent of the soul through eight “views” or stages of yoga, correlating them with broader Jain schemes of spiritual development and with the eightfold pattern familiar from Patañjali’s Yoga system. Modern scholarship on Haribhadra emphasizes that he borrows, compares, and reorders material from Jain, Buddhist, Hindu, and yogic traditions, while retaining a Jain ontology of conscious soul and karmic bondage.

Historically, then, Haribhadra belongs neither to a purely “religious” nor a purely “philosophical” category in the modern Western sense. In classical Indian terms, philosophy of mind is bound to epistemology, liberation theory, metaphysics, moral psychology, contemplative discipline, and debate about the self. Haribhadra’s importance lies in his attempt to integrate these into a comparative, practice-oriented account of consciousness.

2. Jain background: the metaphysical frame of mind

The Jain background is essential. Jain philosophy holds that living beings are embodied conscious selves, or jīvas, whose consciousness is obstructed by karmic matter. The goal is to disentangle the self from karmic bondage through right view, right knowledge, and right conduct, culminating in liberation and omniscience. Jain philosophy also emphasizes ahiṃsā or nonviolence, anekāntavāda or non-one-sidedness, and aparigraha or non-attachment as central ethical and epistemic disciplines.

In Jain metaphysics, karma is not merely a psychological tendency or divine bookkeeping. It is treated as a subtle material reality that adheres to the self through passions and actions. Passions such as attachment, aversion, anger, pride, deceit, and greed function as the adhesive conditions of bondage. Thus mental defilement is neither purely mental nor purely physical; it is a psychophysical and moral condition. The self is active, responsible, and affected by karmic matter, in contrast to systems such as Sāṃkhya, where the puruṣa is a passive witness and prakṛti does the acting.

This is decisive for Haribhadra. His philosophy of mind is not substance dualism in the simple Western sense of “immaterial mind versus material body.” It is a pluralist realism: there are many conscious selves, there is non-conscious matter, there is karmic matter, and there are real interactions of bondage and purification. Mind is therefore not an isolated mental theater; it is the lived, karmically conditioned operation of a conscious self.

3. The basic ontology of mind

Haribhadra’s reconstructed ontology of mind rests on four levels.

First, there is the jīva, the conscious self. The self is not a fiction, bundle, stream, or merely practical convention. It is a real enduring entity.

Second, there are the self’s intrinsic capacities: knowledge, perception, consciousness, and ultimately the capacity for unobstructed omniscience. These are not imported into the self from outside.

Third, there are the self’s modes or operations: actual episodes of cognition, attention, affect, intention, delusion, discipline, and realization. These are changeable.

Fourth, there are karmic and bodily conditions: the senses, the body, mental apparatus, karmic veils, passions, and habits that shape how consciousness manifests.

This means Haribhadra’s account avoids two extremes. It is not materialist, because consciousness is not reducible to body or matter. It is also not a static spiritual essentialism in which the soul is merely untouched and nothing real happens. The soul is intrinsically conscious, but its empirical modes are genuinely transformed through karmic bondage and spiritual discipline. Jain many-sided ontology explains this by distinguishing substance and modes: a substance can persist while its modes change.

This is one of the most philosophically powerful features of the system. Haribhadra can say: the self is permanent enough to be the bearer of responsibility, memory, liberation, and bondage; but it is changeable enough for ignorance, discipline, purification, and liberation to be real. The self is neither absolutely static nor momentary. It is pariṇāmī nitya, enduring through transformation.

4. Consciousness and knowledge

For Haribhadra, consciousness is not a passive glow detached from knowledge. He explicitly criticizes the Sāṃkhya tendency to distinguish consciousness from knowledge by assigning practical cognition to buddhi or mind while keeping puruṣa as pure witness. Haribhadra’s objection is sharp: if consciousness is the essential nature of the soul, then a consciousness free from veiling factors should know. A liberated soul is not an inert witness but purified consciousness that knows all knowables.

This gives Haribhadra a theory of consciousness quite different from many Western debates. In much modern philosophy of mind, consciousness is treated as a puzzle because it seems difficult to explain how subjective experience arises from physical processes. In Haribhadra’s framework, consciousness does not arise from non-conscious matter. It is primitive or basic to the self. The major explanatory problem is instead: Why is consciousness finite, obscured, affectively distorted, and epistemically fragmented?

His answer is karmic and ethical. Knowledge-obscuring, perception-obscuring, deluding, and obstructive karmas veil the self’s capacities. As bondage is reduced, knowledge expands. When karmic veils are removed, the self’s consciousness is unobstructed. Jain sources identify delusory, knowledge-obstructing, perception-obstructing, and obstacle karmas as especially damaging to the self’s cognitive and spiritual functioning.

So Haribhadra’s philosophy of mind transforms the “hard problem” into what might be called the obstruction problem: not “How does matter generate consciousness?” but “How does intrinsically conscious life become occluded, and by what disciplines can it become transparent to itself and reality?”

5. Mind as karmically conditioned operation

Haribhadra’s Yogabindu describes vṛttis as gross and subtle operations of the soul. These operations arise in relation to foreign elements: karmic matter, bodily apparatus, sense conditions, and the practical structures of embodied existence. The task of spiritual discipline is not merely to suppress thoughts but to remove the soul’s capacity for karmic connection.

This is a crucial point. Haribhadra does not reduce mental operations to the body, but he also does not treat ordinary mental life as the pure soul. Ordinary mind is the soul in a conditioned mode. It is consciousness filtered through karmic and bodily apparatus. The mind is not identical with the liberated self; it is the empirical, operational, embodied, and karmically inflected form of the self’s cognitive life.

That is why liberation eventually involves the cessation of bodily, vocal, and mental activity in the state called śaileśī, while the liberated soul itself remains. The cessation of empirical mental operations is not annihilation of consciousness; it is the end of karmically conditioned mental functioning.

This is one of Haribhadra’s deepest departures from modern assumptions. In much contemporary philosophy, the extinction of mental activity would look like the extinction of mind. For Haribhadra, the cessation of conditioned mental activity reveals rather than destroys the true nature of consciousness.

6. Cognition: from sense-based awareness to non-delusion

Haribhadra distinguishes different levels of understanding. A particularly important triad is buddhi, jñāna, and asaṃmoha.

Buddhi is sense-based, object-directed, and tied to ordinary worldly cognition. It can be intelligent, but it remains embedded in sensory orientation and worldly motivation.

Jñāna is cognition informed by scripture, reflection, and right teaching. It is not mere sensory acquaintance; it is interpretive and doctrinally disciplined.

Asaṃmoha, non-delusion, is cognition transformed by correct practice. It is not just knowing propositions but seeing and living without the distortions of attachment, aversion, and confusion. Haribhadra’s account emphasizes that the same external action can have different spiritual results depending on the mental attitude and level of understanding behind it.

This triad is extremely important for philosophy of mind because it refuses to separate cognition from existential orientation. In Haribhadra’s model, cognition is not merely the representation of objects. Cognition has a karmic quality, an affective tone, a practical orientation, and a liberative or binding consequence.

A modern analogy would be to say that Haribhadra does not have a purely “information-processing” theory of mind. He has an ethically valenced theory of cognition. How one knows affects what kind of knower one becomes.

7. Intentionality and the structure of mental directedness

In contemporary philosophy, “intentionality” means the aboutness or directedness of mental states: beliefs, desires, perceptions, hopes, fears, and intentions are about objects, properties, or states of affairs. This has been a central topic in modern philosophy of mind since Brentano and later analytic and phenomenological discussions.

Haribhadra’s framework has an implicit theory of intentionality, though not in that terminology. Mental states are object-directed: one desires, fears, clings to, misperceives, studies, contemplates, or renounces something. But for Haribhadra, aboutness is insufficient as a mark of mind. The crucial question is not only “What is the mind directed toward?” but “With what karmic and affective orientation is the mind directed?”

Attachment-directed cognition binds. Aversion-directed cognition binds. Deluded cognition binds. Scripturally informed and ethically disciplined cognition can loosen bondage. Non-deluded cognition liberates. The intentional object matters, but so do the passions, habits, and existential posture through which the object is apprehended.

This is a major contrast with much modern philosophy of mind. A contemporary theory might ask whether a belief has representational content, whether it is true, and what causal role it plays. Haribhadra asks all of that implicitly, but adds: Does this cognition arise from delusion? Does it increase attachment? Does it purify the self? Does it conduce to liberation?

8. Affect, passion, and moral psychology

Haribhadra’s philosophy of mind is inseparable from affective psychology. The fundamental mental poisons are not merely false beliefs but affectively charged orientations: attachment, aversion, pride, anger, greed, deceit, delusion, and infatuation. These are not optional additions to cognition; they are among the principal mechanisms by which cognition becomes distorted and karmically binding.

In Yogabindu, Haribhadra emphasizes the cultivation of friendliness, joy, compassion, and neutrality toward different classes of beings. These are not sentimental virtues. They are disciplined affective orientations that reshape cognition and reduce karmic entanglement. A mind trained in friendliness, compassion, and equanimity perceives and responds differently from a mind dominated by hostility, envy, or attachment.

Haribhadra also criticizes the idea that egoism is solved simply by denying the existence of the self. In his response to no-self theories, he argues that infatuation and craving are caused not merely by belief in self but by deluding karmic factors. Even a momentary cognition could take itself as “I” if the corresponding delusion is present. Therefore, the cure is not a metaphysical denial of self but the destruction of delusion.

This is philosophically subtle. Haribhadra separates two things often conflated: belief in a self and egocentric delusion. A realist theory of self need not produce egoism; a no-self theory need not automatically remove egoism. The problem is the affective-karmic structure of appropriation, not the bare ontological claim that a self exists.

9. Agency, karma, and freedom

Haribhadra avoids both fatalism and naïve voluntarism. Yogabindu states that fate and perseverance are both relevant: fate is past karma, while present effort is one’s current activity. Present action can alter the trajectory of the soul, but it does so within conditions shaped by past action.

This gives him a layered theory of agency. The self is responsible because it acts, intends, responds, and undergoes consequences. Yet it is not absolutely free in the sense of being unconditioned by its past. Its present mental life is structured by accumulated karmic dispositions. Discipline is therefore necessary because ordinary volition is already conditioned.

For philosophy of mind, this is a powerful model of constrained agency. Haribhadra’s agent is neither a mechanical puppet nor an unconditioned chooser. The self is an active center of consciousness whose current freedom is impaired by past karmic formations but can be enlarged through right cognition, affective discipline, and conduct.

10. Selfhood and personal identity

Haribhadra’s theory of self is developed partly through critique of Buddhist no-self and momentariness. He argues that if there is no enduring self, deliberation about bondage and release becomes incoherent: who is bound, who practices, who realizes, who teaches, and who learns? He also argues that illusion requires some substratum; a dream requires a dreamer. A momentary soul cannot meaningfully produce a later soul unless some continuity is admitted.

Yet Haribhadra does not adopt an absolutely unchanging self in the Sāṃkhya sense. Jainism rejects both absolute eternalism and absolute momentariness. The soul persists as a substance while its modes change. It can be worldly, disciplined, purified, and liberated without ceasing to be itself.

This makes Haribhadra especially interesting in comparison with Western personal identity debates. Hume’s bundle theory denies a deeper substantial unity of the mind, treating it as a bundle of perceptions connected by resemblance, succession, and causation. Haribhadra takes almost the opposite position: a mere bundle cannot account for karmic responsibility, liberation, or sustained practice. But he also avoids a crude immobile substance theory by making the self dynamically transformable.

In contemporary terms, Haribhadra offers a substance-through-process model. The self is real, but its empirical personality is not fixed. It is a continuous conscious substance whose modes can be morally and cognitively reconfigured.

11. Embodiment and the mind-body relation

Haribhadra’s mind-body theory is not Cartesian. Descartes’ dualism famously distinguishes thinking substance from extended substance, and that distinction becomes the standard starting point for the modern Western mind-body problem. Haribhadra also distinguishes consciousness from matter, but the structure of the problem is different.

For Haribhadra, the central relation is not simply mind versus body. It is soul, karmic matter, body, senses, mental operations, passions, and liberation. The body is real. The senses are real. Karmic matter is real. Embodied mental life is real. But the self is not reducible to any of them.

This yields a distinctive answer to the mind-body problem. The body does not generate consciousness; rather, embodied and karmically conditioned structures modulate the manifestation of consciousness. Mental operations arise through the self’s connection with foreign elements, but the self’s intrinsic consciousness is not produced by those elements.

This is not physicalism. Physicalism, in contemporary terms, is the thesis that everything is physical, or that apparently nonphysical phenomena ultimately bear the relevant dependence relation to the physical. Haribhadra would reject that. Yet his system is also not an immaterialism that ignores embodiment. The embodied condition is constitutive of ordinary cognition, bondage, and practice. In that respect, Haribhadra is closer to embodied and enactive approaches than to purely disembodied intellectualism, though he remains metaphysically committed to an enduring conscious self beyond the body. Contemporary embodied cognition emphasizes the role of the body and bodily interaction with the environment in cognition; Haribhadra likewise treats ordinary cognition as inseparable from embodied and practical conditions, but he does not reduce cognition to organism-environment interaction.

12. Yoga as mental development

Haribhadra’s yoga is not merely posture, concentration, or meditative technique. He defines yoga by its final function: it is what leads to mokṣa. As a Jain, he identifies the path with right faith, right knowledge, and right conduct, but he deliberately presents this in a way that can engage multiple traditions rather than merely repeat sectarian formulas.

The Yogadṛṣṭisamuccaya presents eight yogic “views” or stages. These correspond in part to Patañjali’s eight limbs but are also fitted into Jain spiritual development. The result is a developmental psychology of consciousness: the mind moves from outward, sense-bound, passion-governed orientation toward inwardness, discernment, discipline, non-delusion, and finally liberation.

This developmental aspect is central. Haribhadra does not ask only what mind is. He asks what mind can become. A complete philosophy of mind, for him, must include:

what consciousness essentially is;

how cognition becomes obscured;

how affect distorts cognition;

how conduct reinforces or weakens bondage;

how disciplined attention transforms mental operations;

how right understanding matures into non-delusion;

and how liberated consciousness differs from empirical mind.

In modern terms, Haribhadra’s psychology is simultaneously metaphysical, ethical, contemplative, and developmental.

13. The liberated mind: omniscience and non-annihilation

Haribhadra rejects two opposed views of liberation. Against Buddhist no-self interpretations, he denies that liberation is the disappearance of any real self. Against Sāṃkhya-style absolute purity, he denies that bondage is merely illusory or that the self is an inactive witness untouched by real transformation. Jain liberation preserves the self while removing its karmic disease. The worldly condition disappears, but the liberated soul remains.

The liberated self is not a mind in the ordinary empirical sense. It no longer depends on body, senses, passions, karmic veils, or discursive operations. But it is not unconscious. It is purified consciousness, omniscient and free. Haribhadra’s argument against Sāṃkhya is especially important here: if consciousness is truly unobstructed, then it should know. Liberation is not blankness. It is the full actualization of consciousness as knowledge.

This gives Haribhadra a maximalist theory of cognition. Ordinary cognition is partial because of obstruction; liberated cognition is total because the obstructions have been removed. Omniscience is not a supernatural add-on but the logical endpoint of his theory of consciousness.

14. Anekāntavāda and meta-cognition

Haribhadra’s philosophy of mind also includes an epistemic discipline: anekāntavāda, the Jain doctrine of non-one-sidedness. Jain philosophy argues that reality has multiple aspects and that finite knowers grasp reality partially. Substance and mode, permanence and change, identity and difference can all be true from different standpoints when properly qualified.

In Haribhadra, this becomes a philosophical method. His doxographical works do not simply dismiss rival traditions. They classify, compare, criticize, and often reinterpret them according to whether they support liberation, non-delusion, and coherent metaphysics. His Yogabindu even insists that teachings aimed at the same liberative end need not differ in essence, and it cautions against partisan attachment to one’s own tradition.

This is not relativism. Haribhadra is not saying all views are equally true. He is saying finite cognition is perspectival and that philosophical disagreement must be handled with disciplined, non-partisan analysis. The fully unobstructed standpoint is omniscience; finite minds must reason, compare, qualify, and practice humility.

In modern language, Haribhadra has a meta-cognitive theory of philosophical error. One-sidedness is not only a logical mistake; it is a mental and ethical defect. Attachment to one’s view is itself a form of bondage.

15. Contrast with Buddhist philosophy of mind

The central contrast with Buddhism concerns the self. Buddhist philosophy generally refuses to posit a substantial enduring self behind mental and physical processes. Buddhist analyses of mind develop sophisticated accounts of momentary mental events, causal series, intentionality, and consciousness without grounding them in a permanent self.

Haribhadra accepts Buddhist insights into delusion, discipline, and the need to overcome egoistic appropriation, but he rejects the no-self conclusion. His critique is not merely dogmatic. He argues that practice, bondage, release, memory, teaching, and realization require a continuing subject. Without such a subject, the path risks becoming unintelligible.

The deeper difference is this: Buddhist philosophy often treats the sense of self as a construction to be deconstructed, while Haribhadra treats egoistic self-identification as a distortion of a real self. For Buddhism, liberation involves seeing through self-substantialization. For Haribhadra, liberation involves purifying the real self from karmic delusion.

There is convergence, however. Both traditions treat ordinary identity as deluded, both see craving and aversion as central sources of suffering, both develop contemplative psychology, and both deny that ordinary sensory consciousness is epistemically adequate. The disagreement is about whether liberation reveals no-self or purified self.

16. Contrast with Sāṃkhya and Yoga

Haribhadra converges with Sāṃkhya-Yoga in accepting discipline, detachment, meditative refinement, and a strong distinction between consciousness and material nature. But he rejects key Sāṃkhya assumptions.

In Sāṃkhya, puruṣa is pure consciousness, while practical cognition is often assigned to buddhi, a product of prakṛti. Haribhadra objects that separating consciousness from knowledge makes liberated consciousness inert. If consciousness is unveiled, it must be capable of knowledge.

He also differs from Patañjali’s Yoga in the final interpretation of liberation. Haribhadra can appropriate the structure of yogic stages, but he reinterprets the goal through Jain categories: the liberated self is not simply isolated witnesshood but karmically purified omniscient soul. His use of Patañjali is therefore assimilative but not submissive.

The contrast is philosophically significant. Haribhadra’s self is not merely a spectator. It is an active, responsible, transformable conscious being. This is one reason his philosophy of mind has a more integrated moral psychology than classical Sāṃkhya’s sharper split between witness-consciousness and material cognition.

17. Contrast with Vedānta and non-dual traditions

Haribhadra can sound close to Vedānta when he speaks of liberation, inwardness, and the purification of consciousness. His Yogadṛṣṭisamuccaya even notes that different traditions use different names for liberation, such as sadāśiva, paraṃ brahma, or siddhātmā, while treating the liberative aim as one in essence.

But the metaphysical difference remains large. Jainism is pluralist: there are many selves. Liberation does not dissolve individuality into a single absolute. Nor is the world simply illusion. Karmic bondage is real, embodied action is real, and the liberated soul remains itself. Haribhadra’s ecumenism is therefore not metaphysical monism. He can recognize convergences of practice and soteriological aim while still defending Jain pluralism.

18. Comparison with Plato

In ancient Greek philosophy, “soul” is not exactly equivalent to the modern “mind.” Plato’s soul is cognitive, ethical, motivational, and political; it reasons, desires, and must govern bodily appetites. The soul is therefore a larger principle than the modern narrow category of mentality.

Haribhadra converges with Plato in treating the soul as the locus of cognition and ethical transformation. Both oppose the reduction of human life to bodily appetite. Both think the soul can be ordered or disordered. Both make philosophy a discipline of psychic purification.

But the differences are decisive. Haribhadra’s account is karmic, pluralist, and non-theistic in the Jain sense. The soul’s impurity is explained not primarily by the body’s irrational appetites but by karmic bondage and passions. The goal is not contemplation of Forms but the destruction of karmic obstruction and attainment of omniscient liberation.

19. Comparison with Aristotle

Aristotle’s psychology treats the soul as the form or organizing principle of the living body, not as a separable Cartesian mind. His hylomorphic view avoids both reductive materialism and Platonic substance dualism.

Haribhadra is closer to Aristotle than to Descartes in one respect: mind is not treated as an isolated inner theater. Mental life is embedded in embodied activity, capacities, and development. But Haribhadra sharply diverges from Aristotle because the Jain self can be liberated from the body and persist as a perfected conscious being. Aristotle’s soul is typically the form of a living organism; Haribhadra’s jīva is a conscious substance whose embodiment is contingent and karmically conditioned.

So Haribhadra combines a developmental and embodied account of empirical mind with a much stronger doctrine of the separability and persistence of the conscious self.

20. Comparison with Descartes

The comparison with Descartes is tempting but must be handled carefully. Descartes makes the modern mind-body problem canonical by distinguishing thinking substance from extended substance. Haribhadra also distinguishes consciousness from matter, so both are anti-reductionist and dualist in a broad sense.

But Haribhadra is not Cartesian. Cartesian mind is defined by thought; Jain self is defined by consciousness, knowledge, perception, agency, karmic vulnerability, and liberative potential. Cartesian dualism faces the problem of how two radically different substances interact. Haribhadra’s system has a different architecture: karmic matter, passions, bodily activities, and mental operations form a complex theory of bondage. The problem is not an abstract causal gap between mind and body but a concrete account of how soul becomes entangled with karmic matter.

Descartes’ mind-body problem asks: how can mind and matter interact? Haribhadra’s soul-karma problem asks: how can conscious self become bound by subtle matter, and how can it stop attracting and retaining that matter?

21. Comparison with Hume and Kant

Hume’s bundle theory treats the mind as a bundle of perceptions without deeper unity. Haribhadra’s critique of no-self and momentariness would apply strongly against such a view. A bundle of perceptions cannot, on his terms, ground karmic continuity, responsibility, bondage, liberation, or the disciplined ascent from delusion to omniscience.

Kant is more complex. Kant emphasizes the unity of consciousness, the “single common subject” of experience, and the mind’s synthesizing functions; his model of mind is also described as functional in ways that anticipate later cognitive science. Haribhadra would agree that experience requires unity and that cognition is actively structured, not merely passively received. But Kant does not claim theoretical knowledge of the self as a metaphysical substance in the way Haribhadra does. Haribhadra is metaphysically bolder: the self is real, enduring, karmically bound, and liberable.

Thus, in a rough comparative map: Hume is too deflationary for Haribhadra; Kant is closer in recognizing unity and synthesis, but too epistemically restrained about the metaphysical self.

22. Comparison with contemporary physicalism

Contemporary physicalism holds, broadly, that everything is physical or depends in the relevant way on the physical. Haribhadra’s system is incompatible with physicalism because consciousness is intrinsic to jīva and cannot be reduced to matter, body, brain, or behavior.

But Haribhadra does not simply oppose matter to mind. He assigns matter an important role in mental limitation. Karmic matter obstructs knowledge and perception; bodily and sensory apparatus shape empirical cognition; passions bind the self to material karmic influx. So Haribhadra is anti-physicalist about consciousness but not dismissive of material conditions.

A contemporary philosopher might describe his view as a form of non-reductive karmic dualism: consciousness is ontologically basic to selves, but empirical mental life is profoundly conditioned by material and quasi-material factors.

23. Comparison with functionalism

Functionalism identifies mental states by their causal roles: their relations to inputs, outputs, behavior, and other mental states, rather than by their internal constitution. Haribhadra partly converges with this because he classifies mental states by what they do. Buddhi binds when it remains sense-based and passion-governed. Jñāna becomes liberative when informed by scripture and practice. Asaṃmoha produces liberation because it eliminates delusion. Affective states are identified partly by their consequences for bondage or purification.

But Haribhadra is not a functionalist. Mental states are not exhausted by causal role. Their significance depends on the nature of the conscious self, the karmic quality of the state, the truth or falsity of its orientation, and its place in the path to liberation. Two states that are behaviorally similar can be spiritually and cognitively different if one arises from attachment and the other from non-delusion.

So Haribhadra anticipates one functionalist insight—mental states are intelligible through their roles in a system—but embeds that insight in a metaphysics of self and karma that functionalism lacks.

24. Comparison with phenomenology and intentionality theory

Modern phenomenology and intentionality theory emphasize consciousness as directed, structured, and first-personal. Contemporary philosophy defines intentionality as the power of mental states to be about or represent things. Haribhadra shares the concern with directed consciousness but adds a soteriological criterion: mental directedness can be binding or liberating.

The closest phenomenological analogue is the idea that consciousness is not neutral observation but world-involvement shaped by affect, attention, and self-relation. Haribhadra would agree that the way the world appears depends on the structure of consciousness. But he would add that consciousness is structured by karma and passion, not merely by intentional horizons, embodiment, or temporality.

25. Comparison with embodied cognition

Embodied cognition rejects the idea that cognition is only computation in the brain and emphasizes the role of the body and bodily interaction with the environment in cognitive abilities. Haribhadra’s account of empirical mind converges with this at the practical level: ordinary cognition is embodied, sense-mediated, action-involving, and shaped by conduct.

The divergence is metaphysical. Embodied cognition usually remains naturalistic; Haribhadra’s view is karmic and soteriological. For him, embodiment is not the final condition of mind but a temporary condition of bound consciousness. The body is central to ordinary cognition, but liberation transcends embodied cognition altogether.

Thus Haribhadra can be read as offering a two-level model: empirical cognition is embodied and karmically conditioned; ultimate consciousness is disembodied, unobstructed, and omniscient.

26. A systematic reconstruction

Haribhadra’s philosophy of mind can be reconstructed as the following system.

1. Consciousness is intrinsic to the self. The jīva is not a product of matter. It is a conscious substance whose essential capacities include knowledge and perception.

2. Ordinary mind is conditioned consciousness. Empirical mental life consists of the soul’s operations under karmic, bodily, sensory, affective, and social conditions.

3. Cognitive limitation is obstruction, not essence. Ignorance does not prove that consciousness is weak by nature. It proves that consciousness is veiled.

4. Mental states are morally charged. A cognition is not assessed only by representational accuracy. It is also assessed by whether it arises from attachment, aversion, delusion, discipline, or non-delusion.

5. Affect and cognition are inseparable. Passions distort knowing; cultivated affects such as friendliness and compassion purify the mind.

6. Agency is conditioned but real. Past karma shapes present consciousness, but present effort can redirect the soul’s future.

7. The self is enduring but transformable. The soul persists through changing modes. This preserves responsibility and liberation without making change illusory.

8. Spiritual practice is cognitive transformation. Yoga is not external ritual alone; it is the disciplined reconfiguration of attention, affect, conduct, and understanding.

9. Liberation is purified consciousness. The goal is not annihilation, blankness, or mere passivity. It is the cessation of karmic bondage and the full manifestation of knowledge.

10. Philosophical humility is part of mental purification. One-sided views arise from finite and attached cognition. Many-sided analysis disciplines the mind and prepares it for higher knowledge.

27. Haribhadra’s place in the global history of philosophy of mind

Haribhadra deserves a place in the global history of philosophy of mind because he offers a developed alternative to several dominant Western trajectories.

Against materialism, he argues that consciousness is basic and irreducible.

Against Cartesian dualism, he refuses to isolate mind from ethical, karmic, bodily, and practical conditions.

Against bundle theories, he defends an enduring subject of experience.

Against static substance theories, he insists that the self undergoes real modal transformation.

Against purely representational theories of mind, he makes affect, conduct, and liberation central to cognition.

Against purely private theories of consciousness, he ties mental life to nonviolence, compassion, social conduct, and the treatment of other living beings.

Against relativism, he develops perspectival humility without abandoning truth.

His most original contribution, when translated into contemporary terms, is the idea that philosophy of mind must be a philosophy of the purification of consciousness. Mind is not only something to be described; it is something to be disciplined, clarified, and transformed. Haribhadra’s account therefore integrates metaphysics, epistemology, ethics, contemplative psychology, and liberation theory in a way that modern philosophy of mind often separates.

28. Final synthesis

Haribhadra’s philosophy of mind can be named transformative Jain consciousness-realism.

It is realist because the self exists.

It is pluralist because there are many selves and many valid standpoints.

It is non-reductive because consciousness is not produced by matter.

It is embodied at the empirical level because ordinary cognition depends on bodily, sensory, and karmic conditions.

It is developmental because mental life evolves through stages of purification.

It is ethical because passions and conduct shape cognition.

It is soteriological because the final measure of mind is liberation.

It is anti-absolutist because finite knowledge is perspectival.

It is maximalist because unobstructed consciousness culminates in omniscience.

The philosophical center is this: the ordinary mind is not the true measure of consciousness. Ordinary mind is consciousness under bondage. To understand mind fully, one must understand not only perception, thought, desire, and agency, but also delusion, karmic obstruction, disciplined transformation, non-one-sided knowledge, and the possibility of liberated awareness.

PreviousUmāsvāti NextHemacandra

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
